title: What to Do if Your Cat Swallowed a Small Toy | Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Suspect your cat swallowed a toy? Learn about the high urgency level of gastrointestinal blockages and why you must contact a vet immediately.
content_html:
What Is Gastrointestinal Foreign Body Ingestion?

When a cat swallows a small toy, it is medically referred to as a foreign body ingestion. Unlike food, these objects cannot be broken down by the digestive system. Depending on the size, shape, and material of the toy, it can become lodged in the esophagus, stomach, or intestines, leading to a partial or complete gastrointestinal obstruction.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is considered a veterinary emergency. While some very small, smooth objects may pass on their own, the risk of a life-threatening blockage or internal perforation is significant. You should contact your veterinarian or the nearest emergency animal hospital immediately. Do not wait for symptoms to appear, as a blockage can quickly lead to dehydration, tissue necrosis, or sepsis.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you have a duplicate of the toy or the pieces left behind, take a clear photo to show the veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to assess the object's dimensions, sharp edges, and material. This information is critical for triage because it helps the medical team decide if the object can be retrieved non-surgically via endoscopy or if immediate abdominal surgery is required to save the cat's life.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my cat vomit at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting in a cat at home. Many toys can cause damage to the esophagus or lead to choking if they are brought back up.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a blockage?
faqs.answer: Common signs include repetitive vomiting, loss of appetite, lethargy, abdominal pain, and an inability to hold down water.

faqs.question: Can a toy pass through a cat's system naturally?
faqs.answer: While small, rounded objects may occasionally pass, the risk of them getting stuck is very high. Only a veterinarian can determine if it is safe to monitor the cat or if intervention is needed.
